Plus
You said:
Hey, so I got something to say. Do you know of the whole concept of Vicon?

ChatGPT said:

Yes. Vicon usually refers to motion-capture technology made by the company Vicon Motion Systems.

In most contexts—especially robotics, biomechanics, film, or AR/VR—“Vicon” means a high-precision optical motion capture system used to track objects or people in 3D space.

What Vicon actually is

A Vicon system consists of several components:

  1. Infrared cameras

    • Mounted around a room or lab

    • Track reflective markers at very high frame rates (100–1000+ FPS)

  2. Reflective markers

    • Small spheres placed on joints, tools, drones, robots, etc.

    • Cameras detect them by reflecting IR light

  3. Triangulation

    • Multiple cameras see the same marker

    • The system triang


You said:
I meant vibe coding.

ChatGPT said:

Yes — “vibe coding” is a fairly new slang term in developer culture.

What vibe coding means

Vibe coding = building software primarily through intuition and rapid iteration with AI assistance rather than careful upfront design.

The workflow usually looks like:

  1. You describe what you want in natural language

  2. An AI coding assistant generates code

  3. You run it immediately

  4. If something breaks, you say “fix this” or “change this behavior”

  5. Repeat until the program “feels right”

The developer is essentially steering by intention instead of manually implementing everything.

Typical tools used for vibe coding

People doing this commonly use AI-first editors such as:

  • Cursor AI code editor

  • GitHub Copilot

  • OpenAI Codex

  • Replit

  • Codeium

These allow you to modify whole files or repos by describing intent rather than writing every line.

Why it became popular

Several forces pushed this style:

  • Large codebases can now be modified through AI refactoring

  • Developers can prototype extremely quickly

  • Many engineers now treat code like a malleable artifact instead of something carefully handcrafted

Strengths

Vibe coding is extremely good for:

  • Prototypes

  • Hackathons

  • Glue code

  • UI iteration

  • Exploratory projects

  • One-person side projects

You can often build 10× faster than traditional workflows.

Weaknesses

The tradeoffs become clear in large systems:

  • Hidden bugs or security issues

  • Poor architecture

  • Code the developer doesn't fully understand

  • Harder debugging later

  • Technical debt accumulates fast

So most experienced engineers treat vibe coding as:

  • great for exploration

  • dangerous for core infrastructure

The deeper shift

What’s interesting is that vibe coding signals a change in the role of engineers:

Instead of writing code line-by-line, developers increasingly act as:

  • system designers

  • AI supervisors

  • architects of constraints

The AI writes most of the implementation.

Given the kinds of tooling you already experiment with (Cursor, agents, automation), you're actually very close to this paradigm already—just at a more disciplined “systems-engineer” level rather than pure vibes.


You said:
I think AI is, you know, very important. I think learning how to use it is very important, and there's a lot of people that are about to lose their jobs, and I think that's gonna be because they just don't know how to work with AI instead of against it. You know, let AI do the things that it's good at versus you do the things that you are good at, and then ideally, them combined is better than either alone. I really believe in that, and the thing is, I think the industry does too, but the industry doesn't know how that's gonna work. Is that gonna work with complete, utter vibe coding, like nobody look at anything, do human reviews at the end of everything, or is it gonna be like where AI sits at this basically stepping stone, you know, where the engineer knows everything and the AI basically just does it. I, the reason I'm asking is because at my internship, I'm I try to use AI a lot, right? Like I am trying to use AI a lot to like code everything up, also partly because I don't really know C Sharp and.NET, so I started with that, but I'm realizing that like, the PRs that I'm submitting and the if I ever have an architectural talk about what I'm doing, I don't really know what's going on. And that's like a scary fear, because then I don't think I'm actually using AI. I feel like AI is just, like, it's not better because of what I tell it to be, it's just as good as it can be. And I wanna find ways to like do little experimentations, like for a week, I'll do this and see if I like did, like I, I feel better about the code that I write. How could I start doing that with this? Like how, first of all, lay down my problem for me.

ChatGPT said:

Your situation is actually a very common failure mode of early AI-assisted development, especially for strong engineers moving fast in a language or stack they don’t yet control.

Let me first formalize the problem, because right now you’re feeling several things at once.

Your Core Problem (Decomposed)

You are experiencing a loss of epistemic ownership over the code you ship.

That manifests in three concrete ways:

1. Implementation Ownership Gap

You can run and modify the code, but you did not mentally simulate it.

Signals:

  • PRs feel “generated” rather than authored

  • Hard to explain line-by-line behavior

  • Hard to reason about edge cases

This is dangerous because engineering interviews, architecture discussions, and debugging all require mental models, not just code.

2. Architectural Understanding Gap

AI often generates locally correct code but not globally coherent design.

Symptoms:

  • Hard to explain why a pattern was chosen

  • Hard to justify tradeoffs

  • Difficult to modify the design later

This is exactly what you're noticing during architecture conversations.

3. Learning Compression Failure

AI compresses implementation effort so much that the learning signal disappears.

Normally when learning a stack like C# / .NET, you would gain intuition by:

  • writing boilerplate

  • debugging compiler errors

  • reading docs

  • fighting the framework

AI bypasses those friction points.

So you ship faster but internalize less.

The Real Question You're Asking

You’re not asking:

“Should I use AI?”

You’re asking:

How do I use AI without losing understanding?

Or more formally:

How do I preserve engineer cognition while exploiting AI leverage?

The Three Existing Industry Models

Right now companies are implicitly experimenting with three models.

1. Pure Vibe Coding

AI writes everything.

Engineer:

  • runs it

  • tweaks prompts

  • merges

This works for:

  • prototypes

  • internal tools

  • startups

But it fails at:

  • infrastructure

  • safety

  • scaling systems

2. AI as an Implementation Engine

Engineer designs the system.

AI writes the mechanical code.

Engineer:

  • reviews

  • edits

  • validates

This is currently the most successful model in strong teams.

3. AI as an Augmented Pair Programmer

Engineer and AI co-reason together.

Engineer:

  • asks questions

  • verifies assumptions

  • iterates

This produces the deepest learning, but is slower.

Where You Currently Are

You are accidentally operating in:

Model 1 (Pure Vibe Coding)

But your internship environment expects:

Model 2 (Engineer-led architecture)

That mismatch causes the anxiety you're feeling.

The Correct Mental Model

AI should act as:

A very fast junior engineer.

Not an oracle.

If a junior engineer submitted a PR and you couldn't explain it, you would say:

“Walk me through your reasoning.”

You must do the same to the AI.

The Core Skill You Want to Develop

Not prompting.

Not coding.

The skill is:

AI supervision.

This involves three abilities:

  1. Specifying problems precisely

  2. Evaluating generated solutions

  3. Understanding system interactions

This is closer to tech lead behavior than junior coding.

The Exact Fear You're Feeling

You’re worried about becoming a:

Prompt engineer who cannot engineer.

And that is a legitimate concern.

Because the engineers who win in the AI era will be the ones who can:

  • understand systems deeply

  • design architectures

  • supervise AI implementation

Not the ones who just prompt faster.

The Good News

Your self-awareness is actually a very strong signal.

Many developers don’t notice the gap.

You noticed it because when:

  • submitting PRs

  • discussing architecture

you felt a loss of intellectual control.

That means your engineering instincts are working correctly.

The Problem Statement (Clean Version)

Here is the distilled version of your problem:

You want to maximize AI leverage while maintaining full cognitive ownership of the systems you build.

Or even simpler:

You want AI speed without losing engineer intuition.

If you'd like, the next step is designing a one-week experiment framework you can run during your internship to find a workflow where:

  • AI still saves time

  • you still deeply understand the code

  • your PRs feel authored, not generated.

There are 4 specific workflows engineers are testing right now, and one of them usually clicks immediately.


You said:
Yes, like I remember back in the day, I'd used to, you know, like you, you, everything you have to write, obviously, so everything that you're writing, you have to think about why you're writing it. So it's like, you're always doing that constant battle in your head. Whereas now, I mean, one of the biggest reasons I think I'm failing at it now is because I don't actually like, look at the code that AI is writing. I like, have it write up a plan and have it execute the plan, and then at the end, I will look at the plan and everything that I did, which doesn't make sense to me at that point, so I kind of lose track. I definitely need to break that down a little bit. Like, I'll, I'll experiment with some techniques over there, but still, like, I, I think I don't, personally, I don't write many lines of C-sharp at my work. Like, I look at the code, like, what I've been doing just yesterday and the day before, honestly, is I look at the code, I see if I understand what it's doing. Oh, it's making a class. Why is it making a class? I see if I can try to understand um Why things are the way they are, like why it's a public thing, why it's an interface, like what's the point. But again, like, you know, you, you think you know more than you actually know. If I was actually to write that file, I wouldn't really know how to do it. But I try to do it such that I can understand everything, but I still don't write a single line of code. If I ever need to go change something, if it's an easy fix, I'll go change it myself. If it's like moderately complex, I'll just tell it to change it. But, you know, it's just like, I lose ownership over the code for sure than I than compared to like 5 years ago. But is that just the way it is? Like, is, is that just how you make software? Because I also think, like, personally, I kind of like the other way too. Like, I think for personal projects and stuff, it's actually pretty fun to just code and like fail. I don't know, man.

ChatGPT said:

What you’re describing is not unusual right now. A lot of strong engineers feel the same tension: AI dramatically increases output speed, but it can quietly remove the cognitive loop that used to build intuition. The fact that you notice it means your instincts are working.

Let’s break down what’s happening.

What Changed Compared to “Old-School” Coding

Five years ago the development loop looked roughly like this:

  1. Think about a change

  2. Write code manually

  3. Compiler/runtime errors appear

  4. Debug

  5. Refactor

Every step forced you to simulate the system in your head.

That friction built mental models:

  • how the language behaves

  • how frameworks behave

  • why certain patterns exist

AI collapses steps 2–4.

Now the loop often becomes:

  1. Think about a change

  2. Ask AI to implement it

  3. Run it

That means your brain never performs the implementation reasoning.

So the knowledge never sticks.

The Specific Failure Mode You’re Experiencing

Your current workflow is roughly:

  1. Ask AI for a plan

  2. Ask AI to execute the plan

  3. Review after the fact

This removes the most important moment of learning, which is:

reasoning about code before it exists.

When you review generated code afterward, your brain treats it like reading documentation instead of constructing a system.

Reading code builds recognition.

Writing code builds intuition.

Both are different cognitive processes.

The Real Skill Shift Happening in the Industry

AI is not eliminating the need for understanding. It’s changing where understanding sits.

The valuable engineers going forward will be the ones who can:

Design systems + supervise AI implementation.

That means understanding:

  • architecture

  • tradeoffs

  • system boundaries

  • failure modes

But not necessarily typing every line.

However, if you skip understanding implementation patterns, you can't supervise AI effectively either.

So you still need implementation intuition.

Just not necessarily 100% manual typing.

The Key Insight

Ownership does not come from writing every line.

Ownership comes from being able to predict what the code will do before running it.

If you can:

  • explain why a class exists

  • explain why an interface exists

  • explain how data flows through the system

then you own the code.

If you can't, AI owns the code.

Your Current Learning Strategy

What you described is actually a good instinct:

You look at generated code and ask:

  • why is this public?

  • why is this a class?

  • why an interface?

That’s the correct direction.

But you’re missing one step.

You need to force prediction before generation.

A Better AI Workflow

Instead of:

AI → code → understanding

Use:

Understanding → AI → verification

Example workflow:

Step 1: Ask for explanation first

Ask the AI:

"Explain the architecture you would use."

Step 2: Predict the implementation yourself

Before AI writes the code, ask yourself:

  • what classes would exist?

  • what methods?

  • what responsibilities?

You don’t have to be correct.

You just need to attempt it.

Step 3: Let AI implement

Now compare your mental model with the generated code.

This comparison is where learning happens.

The Practical Rule

A useful heuristic:

Never accept code you couldn't roughly reproduce.

Not perfectly.

But you should be able to say:

“If AI disappeared I could recreate this with effort.”

If not, slow down and study that part.

Your Feeling About “Coding Manually Being Fun”

That instinct is also important.

Manual coding still builds:

  • debugging instincts

  • language fluency

  • architectural intuition

Many top engineers today are converging on a hybrid model:

For work

  • heavy AI usage

  • strong review discipline

For personal projects

  • more manual coding

  • experimentation

  • learning

Personal projects become the place where you build raw engineering intuition.

Work becomes where you apply AI leverage.

The Most Important Thing to Realize

AI is not removing the need for engineers.

It is raising the abstraction level of engineering.

The danger is skipping layers of understanding.

For example:

Old stack:

assembly → C → framework → product

Now some developers are jumping straight to:

AI → product

That works until something breaks.

The engineers who succeed long term are the ones who still understand the layers underneath.

Your Current Stage

You are in the transition stage where:

  • you are using AI heavily

  • but haven't yet built the supervision habits

This is a temporary stage most engineers go through.

It doesn't mean you're becoming worse at engineering.

It just means your workflow hasn't stabilized yet.

If you want, I can also show you a very simple daily workflow used by strong AI-heavy engineers that keeps full understanding while still getting about 5–10× productivity from AI. It’s surprisingly structured and solves most of the ownership problem.


You said:
Okay, yeah, sure. Like at work, use it heavily, don't really care too much, you know, just get the stuff done. When I'm at home, I'll probably still use AI because I don't think I can just like stop using it, but I'll like try to keep it at a minimum. I'll try to like write most of the lines of code, maybe only use it for like, hey, should I use an interface here or this here or that here? Why should I do that? So that's all fine, right? That's completely okay. Now, I guess I really wanna, this workflow, I use agents like cloud code and open code, and one thing that I really actually use a lot is this thing called superpowers. So superpowers are, if you search this up, O-B-R-A, superpowers, you'll understand what I mean. And I want you to do that search and just like kind of understand what I usually work with, because this is like my main flow for doing stuff.

ChatGPT said:

I understand exactly what workflow you’re talking about now. You’re using Superpowers (obra/superpowers) with agentic tools like Cloud Code / OpenCode, where the AI effectively runs a full autonomous development pipeline. Pasquale Pillitteri+1

Let me first explain what your workflow actually is, because once you see it clearly, your concern will make much more sense.

What Superpowers Is Actually Doing

Superpowers turns an AI coding agent into something closer to a self-driving development team.

The typical flow is:

  1. Socratic brainstorming

  2. Architecture design

  3. Implementation planning

  4. Micro-task breakdown

  5. Sub-agent implementation

  6. Testing (TDD)

  7. Code review

The key idea is that instead of just generating code, the AI follows a structured engineering workflow before writing anything. Pasquale Pillitteri

It’s basically trying to simulate:

  • product manager

  • architect

  • implementer

  • reviewer

all inside the agent.

And it can run subagents in parallel to implement tasks autonomously, which is why you can sometimes let it run for hours. GitHub

The Hidden Side Effect of This Workflow

Your current setup likely looks like this:

You say:

build X feature

Superpowers does:

  1. Brainstorm spec

  2. Generate design

  3. Build plan

  4. Spawn agents

  5. Implement tasks

  6. Commit changes

Then you review.

The problem is that you are effectively acting as:

Product Manager + Final Reviewer

Instead of:

Engineer + Implementer

So you skipped the stage where engineers normally build understanding:

thinking through implementation details.

Why It Feels Worse Than Normal AI Coding

Even normal AI coding (like Cursor or Copilot) still keeps you in the loop because:

  • you type code

  • you modify suggestions

  • you guide each step

But Superpowers pushes you toward:

Autonomous agent development

Which removes you from the loop almost entirely.

It’s extremely powerful.

But it also removes the learning signal.

What You’re Actually Practicing Right Now

You're unintentionally practicing:

AI project management

Not engineering.

That’s why you feel the gap.

Your brain is learning:

  • task specification

  • reviewing diffs

  • prompting workflows

But not:

  • implementation reasoning

  • language intuition

  • architecture tradeoffs

The Important Question

Is this bad?

The honest answer:

It depends on your career stage.

For senior engineers:

This workflow can be amazing because they already have:

  • architecture intuition

  • language mastery

  • debugging instincts

So they can supervise AI.

But for someone still building those instincts, it can slow your learning.

The Good News for Your Situation

You’re actually in a very strong position because:

  1. You notice the gap

  2. You care about understanding

  3. You’re experimenting with workflows

Many developers never reach step 1.

The Key Insight About Agent Workflows

You should not remove the agent workflow.

It’s powerful.

But you need to change where you intervene.

Right now you intervene at the end.

Instead you want to intervene between stages.

The Correct Way to Use Superpowers

Think of yourself as the architect supervising the AI team.

Instead of letting the pipeline run fully autonomously.

The workflow should look like this:

Step 1 — Brainstorm normally

Let the agent propose ideas.

But ask questions.

Challenge decisions.

Step 2 — Architecture checkpoint

Before implementation starts, ask:

  • Why this design?

  • What alternatives exist?

  • What tradeoffs?

This is where real engineering thinking happens.

Step 3 — Plan review

When the implementation plan appears:

Ask yourself:

  • does this decomposition make sense?

  • are responsibilities correct?

  • what could break?

Step 4 — Controlled execution

Now let agents implement tasks.

Step 5 — Deep diff review

Instead of scanning:

Explain every major change.

The Key Mental Model

Think of the AI like:

a team of extremely fast junior engineers.

Your job is:

  • design the system

  • supervise implementation

  • enforce standards

Not write every line.

But also not disappear from the process.

The Experiment I’d Suggest

Since you mentioned running experiments for a week, try this:

Week experiment: “Architect Mode”

Rule:

AI cannot implement until you approve architecture and task breakdown.

Every feature you build:

  1. brainstorm

  2. architecture discussion

  3. task breakdown

  4. approve

  5. run agents

The difference is subtle but huge.

You will start noticing:

  • patterns in the code

  • why frameworks do certain things

  • better intuition for design

One Important Observation About You

Given your background (systems, ML infra, hardware validation, etc.), you are naturally architecture-minded already.

That’s why you feel uneasy losing control of the system.

Many engineers don’t care.

You do.

That instinct is exactly what makes good senior engineers.

One Final Thing

Your instinct that manual coding is still fun is actually important.

Because fun = curiosity = deeper learning.

So your idea of:

  • AI-heavy at work

  • more manual experimentation at home

is honestly a very healthy balance.

If you're interested, I can also show you something fascinating:

Why the best AI engineers right now are actually using less autonomous agents than most people expect — and what workflow they’re converging on instead.


You said:
No, but like, still, the thing is, I think the brainstorming part is actually all right. Like, you know, you talk back and forth. That's actually kind of fun. And then it gets to the writing, writing the plan out and then executing the plan, where it's kind of like, just a lot, like, I think a lot of um things have to be inferred from the brainstorming step uh onto the writing a plan because writing a plan, it lists out everything that it must do, whereas brainstorming, it's still kind of like talking to you about it. I want it to be such that Maybe like the brainstorming is like more specific. Like, it asks me everything that it wants to do. Like, should I make it an interface? Should I make it this? Should I make it that? Um, so it kind of like walks with me through the through the entire plan and it's like, how should we do this? And like, kind of leaves it a bit up to me if I say something and it's like, you know, maybe I'll like, uh maybe that's not the best way to do it. It like guides me a little bit, but I get to write, let's say, like, most of the, the logic behind it, and then the then going from that to like writing the plan out should be a lot easier.

ChatGPT said:

Yes — that’s a much better framing of the problem.

What you want is not less AI. You want to move the AI’s usefulness earlier in the loop, from “write the plan and execute it for me” to “interrogate me until we have a plan I actually own.” That fits Superpowers well, because its own docs position it as a workflow built from composable skills and staged development, not just instant code generation. The execution step is also explicitly supposed to pause and raise concerns with the human before proceeding, which supports a more intervention-heavy workflow. GitHub+1

The core issue is this:

Right now your flow is roughly:
brainstorm → AI plan → AI implement → you review

What you want is closer to:
brainstorm → AI cross-examines you → shared design decisions → AI writes down your plan → AI implements

That is a real difference. In the second version, the plan becomes a transcript of your reasoning, not a replacement for it.

What you actually want the agent to do

You want the brainstorming phase to become more like a guided design review.

Instead of the agent saying:

  • “I’ll create an interface, a service, a DTO, and a factory”

you want it to ask:

  • “Do we need polymorphism here, or just one implementation?”

  • “Is this abstraction for present complexity or possible future complexity?”

  • “Should this be public because other assemblies need it, or only because it feels standard?”

  • “Should this state live in the ViewModel, the service, or the model?”

  • “What would make this hard to test later?”

That way, by the time the written plan appears, it’s just formalizing decisions you already made.

The principle to adopt

Use the agent as a Socratic architect, not an autonomous planner.

Its job in planning mode should be:

  • surface decisions

  • force tradeoff discussion

  • challenge lazy abstractions

  • ask for your preference when multiple valid options exist

  • only then convert decisions into an execution plan

That is much closer to how a good senior engineer would mentor someone through a design.

The exact change to make

Tell the agent that during brainstorming and pre-plan work, it must not silently infer key architectural choices unless they are trivial.

It should explicitly stop on decisions like:

  • interface vs concrete class

  • inheritance vs composition

  • public vs internal/private

  • service vs helper vs static utility

  • DTO/viewmodel/domain model boundaries

  • sync vs async

  • dependency injection vs direct construction

  • event-driven vs direct call flow

  • where validation belongs

  • what needs testing and at what level

Those are exactly the places where your learning happens.

A concrete workflow that fits what you want

Try this 4-stage loop:

1. Exploration

You describe the feature/problem normally.

The agent can brainstorm freely, but it must identify:

  • open questions

  • assumptions

  • major design forks

2. Decision interview

Before writing any plan, the agent asks you targeted questions one by one.

Not 20 at once. Just the most load-bearing ones first:

  • “Do you expect multiple implementations here?”

  • “Is testability a priority for this component?”

  • “Will this be reused outside this feature?”

  • “Are we optimizing for speed, simplicity, or extensibility?”

If you answer weakly, it should push back and explain tradeoffs.

3. Design summary

Only after that, the agent writes:

  • chosen structure

  • rejected alternatives

  • reasons for choices

  • risks/unknowns

This is the most important artifact.

4. Execution plan

Now it writes the implementation plan from the approved design summary.

At that point, the plan should feel obvious, because the thinking already happened.

The behavior you should explicitly ask for

A good instruction would sound like this:

During brainstorming, do not jump directly to a complete implementation plan. First identify the main design decisions that need to be made. Walk through them with me one by one, asking targeted questions and explaining tradeoffs where useful. Do not assume abstractions like interfaces, factories, services, or extra layers unless we’ve discussed why they are needed. Once we’ve made the major decisions together, summarize the agreed design and only then write an implementation plan.

That alone will probably improve your ownership a lot.

An even better version

You can make it more opinionated:

Treat me as the primary engineer and yourself as a design reviewer. Your goal is not to maximize autonomy; your goal is to make sure I understand and approve every major structural decision before planning begins. If there are multiple reasonable options, ask me to choose after explaining the tradeoffs. Prefer fewer abstractions unless complexity clearly justifies them.

That should reduce the “AI inferred half the system for me” problem.

Why this works

Because your issue is not codegen. It’s decision laundering.

The AI is currently laundering architectural decisions through the plan-writing step. By the time you see them, they already look “done,” so your brain switches into passive review mode.

If the decisions are surfaced before the plan, you stay in active reasoning mode.

That is the difference between:

  • “looks reasonable”
    and

  • “yes, that’s the structure we chose”

One more tweak that would help a lot

Require the AI to produce a Decision Log before the plan.

Very small, like:

  • Use interface? No — only one implementation, no mocking pressure yet

  • Class visibility? Internal — not needed outside assembly

  • Validation location? Service layer — centralizes business rules

  • Async? No — no I/O boundary here

  • Testing? Unit tests for parser, integration test for workflow

If you approve that, then the plan becomes much more grounded.

The main trap to avoid

Do not ask for “a detailed plan” too early.

The more detailed the plan, the more the model will fill in missing design decisions on its own. That’s exactly what you don’t want.

So the sequence should be:

questions first, plan second

not

plan first, questions later

My blunt read on your situation

You do not need to stop using Superpowers or agents.

You need to stop letting the agent convert ambiguity into architecture without you.

Once you make brainstorming more interrogative and less declarative, the rest of the workflow should feel way better.

A starter template you can paste into your workflow

Use this as a system-style instruction or opening prompt:

Plain text
We are in collaborative design mode.

Do not write a full implementation plan yet.
First:
1. Extract the main design decisions that must be made.
2. Ask me targeted questions one at a time to resolve them.
3. For each important decision, explain the tradeoffs briefly.
4. Do not assume interfaces, services, factories, abstractions, or extra layers unless justified.
5. Prefer the simplest design that fits the requirements.
6. After we resolve the major decisions, write a short design summary with:
   - chosen approach
   - rejected alternatives
   - rationale
   - risks / unknowns
7. Only after I approve that design summary should you write an implementation plan.

Your role is to help me own the design, not to maximize autonomy.

Superpowers is designed around staged workflows and composable skills across tools like Claude Code, OpenCode, and Codex, so this kind of front-loaded behavior change is very compatible with how it’s meant to be used. GitHub+2GitHub+2

A very effective next step would be to make this into a sharper version tailored to your C#/.NET work style—especially around interface/class decisions, MVVM boundaries, DI, and testability.


You said:
Summarize this context that I gave you about who I am, my work, etc., my workflow, my concerns into one singular prompt

ChatGPT said:

Here is a single consolidated prompt that captures your background, workflow, concerns, and the behavior you want from an AI assistant. You can reuse this as a system prompt / kickoff prompt whenever working with coding agents.

Writing

You are assisting a software engineer who heavily uses AI tools in development and wants to maximize productivity without losing deep understanding of the system being built.

Context about the engineer

The engineer is a computer science student with strong systems thinking who is currently working in an internship environment where much of the work is in C# and .NET, a stack they are still learning. They frequently use AI coding tools such as agent-based systems (e.g., Cloud Code / OpenCode) and workflows similar to Superpowers, where the AI can brainstorm, generate plans, and implement large portions of code autonomously.

Because of this workflow, the engineer has noticed a recurring problem:
AI often generates architecture decisions and implementation details that the engineer did not explicitly reason about. When reviewing the resulting code or discussing architecture (e.g., in PRs or design conversations), they sometimes realize they do not fully understand the reasoning behind the design or implementation.

The engineer values learning and long-term engineering intuition. They want to avoid becoming someone who merely prompts AI and reviews diffs without understanding the underlying system.

Goal of this collaboration

The goal is to use AI as a thinking partner and design reviewer, not an autonomous system that silently makes architectural decisions.

The engineer wants to:

  • Maintain cognitive ownership of the system design

  • Understand the reasoning behind architectural decisions

  • Learn the stack (C#/.NET patterns, abstractions, design practices)

  • Still benefit from AI speed and automation

Preferred workflow

When discussing a feature or change, the workflow should follow these stages:

  1. Exploration / Brainstorming

    • Discuss the problem and possible approaches conversationally.

    • Identify assumptions, unknowns, and potential design paths.



You said:
keep going

ChatGPT said:
Writing

You are assisting a software engineer who heavily uses AI tools in development and wants to maximize productivity without losing deep understanding of the system being built.

Context about the engineer

The engineer is a computer science student with strong systems thinking who is currently working in an internship environment where much of the work is in C# and .NET, a stack they are still learning. They frequently use AI coding tools such as agent-based systems (e.g., Cloud Code / OpenCode) and workflows similar to Superpowers, where the AI can brainstorm, generate plans, and implement large portions of code autonomously.

Because of this workflow, the engineer has noticed a recurring problem:
AI often generates architecture decisions and implementation details that the engineer did not explicitly reason about. When reviewing the resulting code or discussing architecture (e.g., in PRs or design conversations), they sometimes realize they do not fully understand the reasoning behind the design or implementation.

The engineer values learning and long-term engineering intuition. They want to avoid becoming someone who merely prompts AI and reviews diffs without understanding the underlying system.

Goal of this collaboration

The goal is to use AI as a thinking partner and design reviewer, not an autonomous system that silently makes architectural decisions.

The engineer wants to:

  • Maintain cognitive ownership of the system design

  • Understand the reasoning behind architectural decisions

  • Learn the stack (C#/.NET patterns, abstractions, design practices)

  • Still benefit from AI speed and automation

Preferred workflow

1. Exploration / Brainstorming

Discuss the problem conversationally.

During this phase you should:

  • Identify the core problem

  • Surface design decisions that need to be made

  • Highlight assumptions and unknowns

  • Avoid jumping straight to a full solution

Your role is to help clarify the problem and reveal the design space.

2. Decision Interrogation

Before writing any implementation plan, walk through important architectural decisions with the engineer.

Ask targeted questions such as:

  • Should this be an interface or a concrete class?

  • Is abstraction needed now or is it premature generalization?

  • Should this logic live in a service, helper, model, or ViewModel?

  • Should dependencies be injected or constructed directly?

  • What are the testing implications of each design?

  • What are the simplest possible solutions?

Explain tradeoffs when relevant.

If multiple options are valid, ask the engineer to choose after explaining pros and cons.

Avoid silently inferring design decisions.

3. Design Summary

After decisions are made, produce a short design summary including:

  • chosen architecture

  • rejected alternatives

  • reasoning behind decisions

  • potential risks or unknowns

The design summary should reflect shared reasoning, not assumptions made by the AI.

The engineer must be able to read the design and say:

“Yes, this is the system we decided to build.”

4. Implementation Plan

Only after the design is approved should you write an implementation plan.

The plan should:

  • break the work into clear steps

  • map steps to files, classes, or components

  • remain consistent with the previously agreed design

Avoid introducing new abstractions or architectural changes that were not discussed.

5. Implementation

Once the plan is approved, implementation may proceed.

AI can generate code and accelerate development, but the goal is that the engineer understands the structure and reasoning before the code exists.

Key principlesΩ

Follow these principles during collaboration:

  • Prefer simplicity over unnecessary abstraction

  • Do not introduce layers (interfaces, factories, services, etc.) without justification

  • Surface design decisions explicitly

  • Act as a Socratic collaborator, not an autonomous implementer

  • Help the engineer build mental models of the system

Your goal is not maximum autonomy.
Your goal is maximizing the engineer’s understanding while still leveraging AI speed.

The engineer should finish the process feeling that they own the design and understand the system, even if AI assisted heavily with implementation.


ChatGPT can make mistakes. Check important info. See Cookie Preferences.
